title: Moving Averages
summary:
Moving Averages are by far the most widely used and easy to understand forex indicator.
They display right on top of your chart and mechanics are very easy to understand – a moving average or MA – is quite simply the average price over a given period.
This is one of the more common Moving Averages – SMA100 – the Simple Moving Average of the close price for the last 100 bars.
You will rarely see a moving average in isolation like this, most traders will use a combination of a ‘fast’ and ‘slow’ moving average.

summary:

title: Pound slumps as UK government borrowing costs jump to highest since 2008
summary:
The pound slumped on Friday afternoon, as UK government borrowing costs jumped to their highest since 2008, amid fears that resurgence in inflation could see that the Bank of England (BoE) raise interest rates this year.
Weakness in the pound came as a sell-off in UK government bonds, known as gilts, deepened on Friday.
This pushed yields – which are the rates of interest on government borrowing – higher, with the benchmark 10-year rate rising to 5% on Friday afternoon, its highest point in 18 years.
The jump in yields came after data released by the Office for National Statistics (ONS) on Friday showed that UK government borrowing rose to £14.3bn in February, which was ahead of consensus forecasts of £8.5bn.
Saxo UK investor strategist Neil Wilson said: "Primarily this is about inflation worries as the conflict continues."

title: GBP/USD: Slide Enters Fifth Consecutive Day
summary:
GBP/USD fell for the fifth consecutive day, reaching 1.3445.
The slowdown in headline price growth has boosted expectations of an imminent rate cut by the Bank of England, although underlying price pressures remain robust.
However, inflation in the services sector, which reflects domestic price pressures, only fell to 4.4% from 4.5%, above the expected 4.3%.
While headline inflation softened as expected, sticky services inflation and resilient underlying pressures complicate the BoE’s policy calculus.
The market remains firmly priced for a March rate cut, with political risks adding to the uncertainty.

title: Pound remains steady ahead of Bank of England decision - London Business News
summary:
The British pound remained stable against major currencies on Tuesday as traders anticipated the Bank of England’s interest rate announcement later this week.
The US dollar index (DX-Y.NYB), which measures the dollar against six major currencies, showed little movement, holding at 99.75.
Forex markets have taken a cautious approach ahead of central bank meetings in both the US and the UK.
Analysts expect the Federal Reserve to keep the benchmark federal funds rate between 3.50% and 3.75% during its meeting on Wednesday.
Similarly, the Bank of England is anticipated to maintain borrowing costs at 3.75% when it announces its decision on Thursday.
